thesis: This lecture argues that generating coherent, feature-length AI screenplays requires decomposing the writing task into hierarchical stages, with specialized agents coordinating premise, arc, sequence, and scene generation to overcome one-shot coherence failures and ensure narrative consistency.
scope: Motivating AI-generated screenplays within the entertainment industry
Limitations of one-shot long-form generation: context drift and coherence loss
Hierarchical structure of screenplays (premise, arc, sequences, scenes)
Stage-wise agentic workflow: premise agent, arc agent, sequence agent, scene agent
Inspection of structured JSON outputs at each step
Role and design of enhancement agents for intermediate improvement
Generalizing hierarchical agentic flows to other long-form documents
Hands-on coding and workflow adaptation practices
learning_objectives: Identify and explain why one-shot AI generation fails at long-form coherence due to local vs. global context limitations (Slide 3).
Describe and schematize the hierarchical components of a movie screenplay: premise, story arc, sequences, scenes (Slides 4–5).
Map each agent (Premise Agent, Arc Agent, Sequence Agent, Scene Agent) to its structural role and the input/output JSON artifacts it produces (Slides 6–15).
Analyze the function and impact of enhancement agents on intermediate screenplay outputs and explain where they fit in the workflow (Slide 16).
Apply the hierarchical agent workflow by adapting it to generate other structured long-form documents and recognize analogous stages (Slide 17).
Construct or extend an agentic pipeline for screenplay generation, inserting enhancement stages and transferring the approach to non-script documents (Slide 18).
audience: Students with foundational knowledge of AI text generation who wish to learn advanced, agent-based approaches for scalable, structured, and coherent long-form creative writing.
instructor_strategy: The instructor scaffolds understanding by first highlighting the failure modes of naïve generation (context drift, loss of coherence), then systematically introducing the hierarchical decomposition of screenplays. Each stage—premise, arc, sequence, scene—is illustrated first through process flowcharts and agent role descriptions, then grounded with concrete JSON examples. After presenting the baseline pipeline, the instructor discusses enhancement agents for quality improvement and culminates with adaptation of the workflow to other document types, ending with an applied coding session.
why_this_matters: Long-form text generation by AI often fails due to the inability to maintain global structure and coherence over many pages, leading to narratives that lose focus, consistency, or introduce hallucinations. This lecture matters because it demonstrates how hierarchical, agent-driven workflows—where each agent owns a compositional level of the document—can reliably produce complex, structured works by enforcing progressive planning, modular generation, and explicit context passing. Such decomposition is central to scaling AI creativity, designing robust agentic pipelines, and generalizing the methodology to any domain demanding structured, high-fidelity documents.
pedagogical_focus: Students should leave able to explicitly map and critique each stage of the agentic screenplay pipeline, diagnose points of narrative drift or incoherence, and transfer the hierarchical decomposition approach to other document formats requiring structure, modularity, and staged coherence.
